Week 2: Statistical Inference

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

STAT9004 — Regression Analysis

Published

April 15, 2026

Important⭐ Assessment Reminders
  • This week is a review week — the professor confirmed that all topics covered here form the foundation for everything that comes next in STAT9004, including all linear model assessments.
  • The professor stated directly: “For your assessment as well, [you need to] know the importance of statistical significance and all that.”
  • Key exam-relevant areas identified from the exercise sheet: p-values, Type II errors, sample size calculations, power analysis, confidence intervals, and effect size interpretation.
  • When writing conclusions in any report or assignment, you must quote the p-value and state conclusions in terms of the population parameter.

1 Key Topics

This week covers the following main ideas:

  • Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
  • Population vs. Sample (Parameters vs. Statistics)
  • Probability Sampling
  • Point Estimates and Sampling Error
  • The Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)
  • Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
  • Confidence Intervals
  • Hypothesis Testing: Null and Alternative Hypotheses
  • The p-value and Level of Significance
  • Type I and Type II Errors
  • Statistical Power
  • Effect Size (Cohen’s d and R²)
  • Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance

2 Core Content

2.1 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics

Descriptive statistics simply describe the data you already have. For example, you might calculate the mean exam score or draw a chart. You are not trying to say anything beyond what is in front of you.

Inferential statistics go further. They use methods such as hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and regression analysis to make conclusions about a large group (a population) based on a smaller group (a sample). These methods are grounded in probability theory — we use probability to say how confident we are in our conclusions.

Note💡 Analogy

Think of a soup chef who wants to know if the soup is salty enough. They do not drink the entire pot — they take one spoonful (the sample) and taste it (the statistic). Their judgment about the whole pot is an inference about the population.

Professor’s note: “Statistical inferences are based on a process of reasoning based on probability theory.”

2.2 Population vs. Sample

Term Definition Symbol
Population All individuals under study — the full group you want to learn about \(N\)
Sample A smaller subset taken from the population \(n\)
Parameter A number that describes the population (usually unknown) \(\mu\), \(\sigma\)
Statistic A number calculated from the sample, used to estimate a parameter \(\bar{x}\), \(s\)

In practice, the population is almost always too large to measure fully. We use sample statistics to estimate unknown population parameters.

For the estimate to be reliable, the sample must be collected in an unbiased way.

Professor’s note: “In general, the parameters of a population are unknown. We use statistics to estimate parameters.”

2.3 Probability Sampling

For a sample to be a fair representation of the population, we use probability sampling. This means:

  • Every member of the population has a known, non-zero chance of being selected.
  • Units are chosen at random.
  • All statistical inference methods assume that the sample was collected this way.

If the sample is not random, the estimates may be biased — they may systematically over- or under-estimate the true value.

Warning⚠️ Common Mistake

Students sometimes confuse “random” with “haphazard”. In statistics, random means that every unit has an equal and known probability of selection — it is a mathematical concept, not just picking things carelessly.

2.4 Point Estimates and Sampling Error

From a random sample, we calculate a point estimate — a single number that is our best guess for an unknown population parameter.

For example:

  • The sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is a point estimate of the population mean \(\mu\).

However, because the sample is not the whole population, there will always be a gap between the estimate and the true value. This gap is called sampling error.

\[ \text{Sampling Error} = \hat{\theta} - \theta \]

where \(\hat{\theta}\) is the estimate and \(\theta\) is the true parameter.

2.4.1 Ways to Reduce Sampling Error

Method How It Helps
Increase sample size Larger samples are more representative
Use random/stratified sampling Reduces systematic bias
Calculate the Standard Error of the Mean Quantifies how much error to expect
Use Confidence Intervals Gives a range that accounts for uncertainty

2.5 Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)

The Standard Error of the Mean tells us how much the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is expected to vary from sample to sample. It is calculated as:

\[ \text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \]

Where:

  • \(\sigma\) = population standard deviation
  • \(n\) = sample size
Important⭐ Key Formula

\[\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]

As \(n\) increases, SEM gets smaller — the estimate becomes more precise. This formula appears in confidence intervals, sampling distributions, and power analysis.

Note💡 Analogy

Imagine measuring the height of people in a country. If you measure 5 people, your average could easily be wrong. If you measure 500 people, you are much more likely to be close to the true average. The SEM captures this idea mathematically.

2.6 Sampling Distributions

If you took many different random samples from the same population and calculated the mean for each, those means would form their own distribution — the sampling distribution of the mean.

  • The sample mean is a random variable — it changes each time you take a new sample.
  • If the underlying population follows a Normal distribution with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\):

\[ \bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \]

This means the sampling distribution has:

  • The same mean as the population (\(\mu\))
  • A smaller standard deviation equal to the SEM (\(\sigma / \sqrt{n}\))

2.7 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

Important⭐ One of the Most Important Results in Statistics

Central Limit Theorem (CLT): For large sample sizes (\(n \geq 30\)), the distribution of the sample mean is approximately Normal, regardless of the shape of the underlying population.

\[ \bar{X} \approx N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \quad \text{when } n \geq 30 \]

This is what allows us to use Normal-distribution methods (confidence intervals, z-tests) even when the original data is not normally distributed.

2.7.1 R Code: Demonstrating the CLT

The exercise sheet asks you to simulate rolling a die to see the CLT in action. As the sample size grows, the distribution of the mean becomes more and more bell-shaped.

Show R code 
n_samples <- 10000   # Number of simulated samples
n <- c(1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 1000)  # Sample sizes to try

X <- matrix(0, n_samples, length(n))

par(mfrow = c(3, 2))  # Arrange plots in 3 rows, 2 columns

for (i in n) {
  j <- which(n == i)
  for (k in 1:n_samples) {
    X[k, j] <- mean(as.integer(runif(i, 1, 7)))
  }
  hist(X[, j],
       main = paste("Distribution of the mean of", i, "rolls"),
       freq = FALSE,
       xlim = c(0.5, 6.5))
}

What to observe: With n = 1, the histogram looks flat (uniform). As n increases toward 1000, the histogram becomes a narrow, bell-shaped curve centred at 3.5 (the true mean of a fair die). This is the CLT at work.

2.8 Confidence Intervals

A confidence interval (CI) is a range of values that we are highly confident contains the true population parameter.

2.8.1 The Logic Behind a 95% CI

If we took many samples and built a 95% CI for each one, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true population mean \(\mu\).

For a single sample, we say: we are 95% confident that \(\mu\) lies within two standard errors of \(\bar{x}\):

\[ \bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \]

Important⭐ Why Confidence Intervals Matter
  • A larger sample produces a narrower CI (greater certainty).
  • CIs show the range of plausible effect sizes, not just whether a result is “significant”.
  • They help you distinguish between statistical significance and practical significance.

Professor’s note: “If you provide a confidence interval for the parameter you are estimating, this will estimate the size of an effect. The use of confidence intervals facilitates the distinction between statistical significance and clinical significance.”

2.9 Hypothesis Testing

Hypothesis testing is a formal way of using data (evidence) to answer research questions. It ensures that — in the long run — we reach the correct conclusion a certain percentage of the time.

2.9.1 Steps in Hypothesis Testing

  1. State the hypotheses — null (\(H_0\)) and alternative (\(H_1\)).
  2. Set the significance level (\(\alpha\), usually 0.05).
  3. Collect data and compute a test statistic.
  4. Calculate the p-value.
  5. Make a decision — reject or fail to reject \(H_0\).
  6. State your conclusion in terms of the population parameter, and always quote the p-value.

Professor’s note: “When you are doing any reports or anything, you should always quote your p-value in your conclusions. And remember, when you’re drawing your conclusions, if you’re doing hypothesis testing, your conclusions are about the population parameter that you’re studying.”

2.9.2 The Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\))

The null hypothesis is the starting point — a statement of “no effect” or “no difference”. We assume it is true until the data gives us enough evidence to doubt it.

  • Always stated with an equality sign: \(H_0: \mu = 0\)
  • The statement is made about the population parameter, not the sample statistic.

Professor’s note: “We assume this statement is true until the data indicates otherwise.”

2.9.3 The Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_1\))

The alternative hypothesis is your research theory — what you believe is actually true.

  • It contains all values not covered by the null hypothesis.
  • Together, \(H_0\) and \(H_1\) must cover all possible situations.
Type Hypotheses Called
Two-sided \(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu \neq 0\) Two-tailed test
One-sided (greater) \(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu > 0\) One-tailed test
One-sided (less) \(H_0: \mu = 0\), \(H_1: \mu < 0\) One-tailed test

Professor’s note: “The alternative hypothesis is your research theory. The researcher actually believes this statement to be true.”

2.10 The p-value

The p-value is the result of the significance test. It tells you how likely the null hypothesis is to be true, given the data you observed.

More precisely: it is the probability of observing data at least as extreme as yours, if the null hypothesis were true.

Important⭐ The p-value — Most Important Concept in Hypothesis Testing
  • A small p-value → the null hypothesis is unlikely to be true → we have strong evidence against \(H_0\).
  • Always report the exact p-value in your conclusions.

🎵 Professor’s classroom rhyme (do not write in exams!): “If p is low, null must go.” That is: if \(p \leq \alpha\) (e.g., \(p \leq 0.05\)), reject \(H_0\).

The only two valid conclusions you can write are:

  1. “We reject the null hypothesis.” (when \(p \leq \alpha\))
  2. “We fail to reject the null hypothesis.” (when \(p > \alpha\))

Anything else is incorrect.

Professor’s note: “When I teach hypothesis testing, there’s only two statements that you’re allowed to use. Everything else is incorrect.”

2.11 Level of Significance (\(\alpha\))

The significance level \(\alpha\) is set before the test is run. It is the amount of error you are willing to accept in your conclusion.

\(\alpha\) Meaning
0.05 (5%) Most common — you accept a 5% chance of a Type I error
0.01 (1%) More stringent — used when false positives are very costly
0.001 (0.1%) Very stringent — used in fields like genetics

Decision rule:

  • If \(p \leq \alpha\): Reject \(H_0\) → result is statistically significant
  • If \(p > \alpha\): Fail to reject \(H_0\) → not sufficient evidence

Professor’s note: “This is the degree of uncertainty we are prepared to accept in a conclusion. It depends on your study.”

Warning⚠️ Common Mistakes
  • A significant result does not mean the result is certainly true — only that we have evidence.
  • A non-significant result does not mean the null hypothesis is true — only that we do not have enough evidence to reject it.
  • Never say: “We accept \(H_0\)” or “We accept \(H_1\)”. These are wrong.

2.12 Type I and Type II Errors

Because we base conclusions on sample data, we can sometimes make mistakes.

\(H_0\) is actually TRUE \(H_0\) is actually FALSE
We Reject \(H_0\) ❌ Type I Error (probability = \(\alpha\)) ✅ Correct — this is Power
We Fail to Reject \(H_0\) ✅ Correct ❌ Type II Error (probability = \(\beta\))
  • Type I Error = “False positive” — rejecting a true null hypothesis. You can control this by setting \(\alpha\).
  • Type II Error = “False negative” — failing to detect a real effect. Its probability is \(\beta\). You cannot directly control it without knowing the true population value.
Important⭐ Key Point on Errors

We can control Type I errors by setting \(\alpha\). We cannot directly control Type II errors — because to calculate the exact probability of \(\beta\), we would need to know the true population parameter, which is unknown.

Professor’s note: “We controlled for Type 1 errors by setting the level of significance, your alpha, or your 5%, or your 1%.”

Note💡 Real-World Analogy

A Type I Error is like a false fire alarm — the alarm goes off (you reject \(H_0\)) but there is no fire (no real effect). A Type II Error is like a missed fire — there is a real fire (a real effect), but the alarm does not go off (you fail to detect it).

2.13 Statistical Power

Power is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — of rejecting \(H_0\) when it truly is false.

\[ \text{Power} = 1 - \beta \]

  • High power is desirable — you want your study to have a good chance of finding an effect if one really exists.
  • The trade-off: Decreasing \(\alpha\) (being more careful about Type I errors) automatically increases \(\beta\) (makes Type II errors more likely), which reduces power.

2.13.1 Increasing Power

The best solution to this trade-off is to increase the sample size before the study begins. With enough data, you can keep both \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) at acceptable levels simultaneously.

Show R code 
# Install packages if needed
# install.packages("pwr")
# install.packages("asbio")

library(pwr)
library(asbio)

# Q6: One-sample test — power of detecting a 0.5 hr difference
# sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q6d: Minimum sample size for 90% power, detecting 15-minute (0.25 hr) difference
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, power = 0.9, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.25,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q7a: Two-sample t-test — power, n=20 per group, pooled SD=6.2, effect=5 complaints
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7b: Recalculate with alpha = 0.01
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.01,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7c: Minimum sample size for 90% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.9, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q8c: HbA1c intervention — one-sample, one-sided, 80% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.8, d = (1/2.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "less")

Professor’s note: “We can select a sample size before the study commences so that alpha and beta are both at satisfactory levels.”

2.14 Effect Size

The effect size tells you how big or strong an effect is — separate from whether it is statistically significant.

A result can be statistically significant but practically tiny. The effect size tells you if the result actually matters in the real world.

  • Larger effect size = stronger relationship = more likely to be practically important
  • Associated with every effect size is a standard error (a measure of its uncertainty)
Important⭐ Why Effect Size is Important

Professor’s note: “It’s good in practice to report your effect size. You’re doing it anyway — you don’t know you’re doing it when you’re reporting a confidence interval.”

Reporting a confidence interval is a way of reporting effect size, even if you do not realise it.

2.14.1 Cohen’s d (for t-tests)

Used when comparing two means:

\[ d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}} \]

The difference between two means, divided by the pooled standard deviation. This standardises the difference so it can be compared across studies.

2.14.2 \(R^2\) (for Linear Regression)

In linear regression, effect size is measured by \(R^2\) — the square of Pearson’s correlation coefficient \(r\):

\[ R^2 = r^2 \]

\(R^2\) is the coefficient of determination. It tells you what proportion of the variation in \(Y\) is explained by \(X\).

Important⭐ How to Interpret \(R^2\)

Professor’s note: “If you had a coefficient of determination that was 40%, the interpretation would be: 40% of the variation in Y is explained by the variation in X. Which leaves 60% — that’s unexplained variation, maybe due to something else you haven’t considered.”

Always express \(R^2\) as a percentage when interpreting it, and identify what the unexplained variation might be due to.

2.15 Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance

This is one of the most important distinctions in applied statistics.

Statistical Significance Clinical / Practical Significance
What it means Evidence that an effect exists in the data The effect is large enough to matter in practice
How it is judged p-value vs. \(\alpha\) Effect size
Risk when sample is large Small, trivial effects may be “significant” May still have very small effect size
Risk when sample is small A real effect may not be detected Under-powered study may miss important differences

For a result to be truly meaningful, it must be:

  1. First — statistically significant (\(p \leq \alpha\))
  2. Then — have a meaningful effect size (large enough to matter practically)
Important⭐ The Blood Pressure Example

Professor’s note: “If you’re studying blood pressure — small changes in blood pressure might be practically significant. It depends on the subject that you’re studying.”

In medicine, a statistically significant reduction of even 2 mmHg in blood pressure across a large population could prevent thousands of strokes. So “small” effect sizes can still be practically important — it always depends on context.

Warning⚠️ Common Mistake: Over-relying on the p-value

Professor’s note: “Instead of attaching too much importance on statistical significance, we should pay attention to the actual data as well as the p-value.”

  • A non-significant result does NOT mean there is no effect. It means you did not have enough evidence to detect one (perhaps your sample size was too small).
  • A significant result with a tiny effect may have little practical value.
  • Always interpret both the p-value and the confidence interval together.

3 Exercise Sheet Summary

This table maps the main exercises to the concepts they test.

Question Topic Key Concept
Q1 p-value What exactly does a p-value tell you?
Q2 Type II error Definition of Type II error
Q3 Power & sample size Effect of increasing n on Type II error probability
Q4 Bias Definition of an unbiased estimator
Q5 CLT simulation Visualising how sample mean distribution becomes Normal
Q6 Power analysis One-sample z-test; power calculation by hand and in R
Q7 Power analysis Two-sample t-test; power and minimum sample size
Q8 Power analysis Clinical trial; HbA1c; one-sided test; Type I/II interpretation
Q9 Sample size for CI Margin of error: battery life, \(n\) for 95% CI
Q10 Sample size for CI Margin of error: sugar content, \(n\) for 99% CI
Q11 Sample size for CI Margin of error: flight delays, \(n\) for 99% CI

3.0.1 Sample Size for a Confidence Interval (Questions 9–11)

When you want to estimate a population mean with a specific margin of error \(E\) and confidence level, the required sample size formula is:

\[ n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 \]

Where:

  • \(z_{\alpha/2}\) = critical value (1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%)
  • \(\sigma\) = population standard deviation
  • \(E\) = desired margin of error

Always round up to the nearest whole number.

3.0.2 Practice Question with Answer

Important⭐ Practice: State H₀ and H₁

A company targets a mean order processing time of 6 hours. A sample of 50 orders had a mean of 5.2 hours and standard deviation of 1.35 hours. State the hypotheses.

Answer:

\[H_0: \mu = 6 \text{ hours}\]

\[H_1: \mu \neq 6 \text{ hours}\]

Note: \(H_0\) uses an equality sign and refers to the population mean \(\mu\), not the sample mean \(\bar{x}\).

4 Key Formulas Reference

Formula What It Is
\(\text{SEM} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}\) Standard Error of the Mean
\(\bar{X} \sim N(\mu,\ \sigma/\sqrt{n})\) Sampling distribution (Normal population)
\(\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot (\sigma/\sqrt{n})\) Approximate 95% Confidence Interval
\(\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\) Power of a test
\(d = (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) / s_{\text{pooled}}\) Cohen’s d (effect size for t-tests)
\(R^2 = r^2\) Coefficient of determination (effect size for regression)
\(n = (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^2\) Sample size for estimating a mean

5 Summary Checklist

  • ✅ I can explain the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics.
  • ✅ I can distinguish between a population parameter and a sample statistic.
  • ✅ I know why random (probability) sampling is essential.
  • ✅ I can define and calculate the Standard Error of the Mean (SEM).
  • ✅ I can state the Central Limit Theorem and explain what it means in plain language.
  • ✅ I can explain the logic of a confidence interval.
  • ✅ I can state a null and alternative hypothesis correctly.
  • ✅ I know what a p-value tells me and how to use it to make a decision.
  • ✅ I know the two and only two valid conclusions in hypothesis testing.
  • ✅ I can define Type I and Type II errors and explain which one we can control.
  • ✅ I can define statistical power and explain the trade-off between \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\).
  • ✅ I can explain what effect size is and name two measures: Cohen’s d and \(R^2\).
  • ✅ I can interpret \(R^2\) as a percentage (e.g., “40% of the variation in Y is explained by X”).
  • ✅ I can distinguish between statistical significance and clinical/practical significance.
  • ✅ I always report the p-value and a confidence interval together in my conclusions.

6 Pre-Topic Checklist: Before Moving to Week 3

7 Additional Resources

  • R packages used: pwr, asbio — install with install.packages("pwr") and install.packages("asbio")
  • R script for Exercise 2 is available on Canvas (under Units → Regression Analysis)
  • Fethney (2010) paper on statistical and clinical significance — available in the course materials

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Assessment Reminders

- This week is a **review week** — the professor confirmed that all topics covered here form the **foundation for everything that comes next** in STAT9004, including all linear model assessments.
- The professor stated directly: *"For your assessment as well, [you need to] know the importance of statistical significance and all that."*
- **Key exam-relevant areas identified from the exercise sheet:** p-values, Type II errors, sample size calculations, power analysis, confidence intervals, and effect size interpretation.
- When writing conclusions in any report or assignment, you **must** quote the p-value and state conclusions in terms of the **population parameter**.
:::

---

# Key Topics

This week covers the following main ideas:

- Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
- Population vs. Sample (Parameters vs. Statistics)
- Probability Sampling
- Point Estimates and Sampling Error
- The Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)
- Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
- Confidence Intervals
- Hypothesis Testing: Null and Alternative Hypotheses
- The p-value and Level of Significance
- Type I and Type II Errors
- Statistical Power
- Effect Size (Cohen's d and R²)
- Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance

---

# Core Content

## Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics

**Descriptive statistics** simply describe the data you already have. For example, you might calculate the mean exam score or draw a chart. You are not trying to say anything beyond what is in front of you.

**Inferential statistics** go further. They use methods such as **hypothesis testing**, **confidence intervals**, and **regression analysis** to make conclusions about a large group (a **population**) based on a smaller group (a **sample**). These methods are grounded in **probability theory** — we use probability to say how confident we are in our conclusions.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy

Think of a soup chef who wants to know if the soup is salty enough. They do not drink the entire pot — they take one spoonful (the **sample**) and taste it (the **statistic**). Their judgment about the whole pot is an **inference** about the **population**.
:::

> *Professor's note:* *"Statistical inferences are based on a process of reasoning based on probability theory."*

---

## Population vs. Sample

| Term | Definition | Symbol |
|------|------------|--------|
| **Population** | All individuals under study — the full group you want to learn about | $N$ |
| **Sample** | A smaller subset taken from the population | $n$ |
| **Parameter** | A number that describes the population (usually **unknown**) | $\mu$, $\sigma$ |
| **Statistic** | A number calculated from the sample, used to **estimate** a parameter | $\bar{x}$, $s$ |

In practice, the population is almost always too large to measure fully. We use sample statistics to **estimate** unknown population parameters.

For the estimate to be reliable, the sample must be collected in an **unbiased** way.

> *Professor's note:* *"In general, the parameters of a population are unknown. We use statistics to estimate parameters."*

---

## Probability Sampling

For a sample to be a fair representation of the population, we use **probability sampling**. This means:

- Every member of the population has a **known, non-zero chance** of being selected.
- Units are chosen **at random**.
- All statistical inference methods assume that the sample was collected this way.

If the sample is not random, the estimates may be **biased** — they may systematically over- or under-estimate the true value.

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common Mistake

Students sometimes confuse "random" with "haphazard". In statistics, **random** means that every unit has an equal and known probability of selection — it is a mathematical concept, not just picking things carelessly.
:::

---

## Point Estimates and Sampling Error

From a random sample, we calculate a **point estimate** — a single number that is our best guess for an unknown population parameter.

For example:

- The **sample mean** $\bar{x}$ is a point estimate of the **population mean** $\mu$.

However, because the sample is not the whole population, there will always be a gap between the estimate and the true value. This gap is called **sampling error**.

$$
\text{Sampling Error} = \hat{\theta} - \theta
$$

where $\hat{\theta}$ is the estimate and $\theta$ is the true parameter.

### Ways to Reduce Sampling Error

| Method | How It Helps |
|--------|-------------|
| Increase sample size | Larger samples are more representative |
| Use random/stratified sampling | Reduces systematic bias |
| Calculate the Standard Error of the Mean | Quantifies how much error to expect |
| Use Confidence Intervals | Gives a range that accounts for uncertainty |

---

## Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)

The **Standard Error of the Mean** tells us how much the sample mean $\bar{x}$ is expected to vary from sample to sample. It is calculated as:

$$
\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

Where:

- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation
- $n$ = sample size

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Key Formula

$$\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

As $n$ increases, SEM gets **smaller** — the estimate becomes more **precise**. This formula appears in confidence intervals, sampling distributions, and power analysis.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy

Imagine measuring the height of people in a country. If you measure 5 people, your average could easily be wrong. If you measure 500 people, you are much more likely to be close to the true average. The SEM captures this idea mathematically.
:::

---

## Sampling Distributions

If you took many different random samples from the same population and calculated the mean for each, those means would form their own distribution — the **sampling distribution of the mean**.

- The sample mean is a **random variable** — it changes each time you take a new sample.
- If the underlying population follows a Normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)
$$

This means the sampling distribution has:

- The **same mean** as the population ($\mu$)
- A **smaller standard deviation** equal to the SEM ($\sigma / \sqrt{n}$)

---

## The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ One of the Most Important Results in Statistics

**Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** For large sample sizes ($n \geq 30$), the distribution of the sample mean is **approximately Normal**, *regardless* of the shape of the underlying population.

$$
\bar{X} \approx N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \quad \text{when } n \geq 30
$$

This is what allows us to use Normal-distribution methods (confidence intervals, z-tests) even when the original data is not normally distributed.
:::

### R Code: Demonstrating the CLT

The exercise sheet asks you to simulate rolling a die to see the CLT in action. As the sample size grows, the distribution of the mean becomes more and more bell-shaped.

```{r clt-demo, eval=FALSE}
n_samples <- 10000   # Number of simulated samples
n <- c(1, 2, 5, 10, 100, 1000)  # Sample sizes to try

X <- matrix(0, n_samples, length(n))

par(mfrow = c(3, 2))  # Arrange plots in 3 rows, 2 columns

for (i in n) {
  j <- which(n == i)
  for (k in 1:n_samples) {
    X[k, j] <- mean(as.integer(runif(i, 1, 7)))
  }
  hist(X[, j],
       main = paste("Distribution of the mean of", i, "rolls"),
       freq = FALSE,
       xlim = c(0.5, 6.5))
}
```

> *What to observe:* With n = 1, the histogram looks flat (uniform). As n increases toward 1000, the histogram becomes a narrow, bell-shaped curve centred at 3.5 (the true mean of a fair die). This is the CLT at work.

---

## Confidence Intervals

A **confidence interval (CI)** is a range of values that we are highly confident contains the true population parameter.

### The Logic Behind a 95% CI

If we took many samples and built a 95% CI for each one, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true population mean $\mu$.

For a single sample, we say: we are **95% confident** that $\mu$ lies within two standard errors of $\bar{x}$:

$$
\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Why Confidence Intervals Matter

- A **larger sample** produces a **narrower** CI (greater certainty).
- CIs show the **range of plausible effect sizes**, not just whether a result is "significant".
- They help you distinguish between **statistical significance** and **practical significance**.

> *Professor's note:* *"If you provide a confidence interval for the parameter you are estimating, this will estimate the size of an effect. The use of confidence intervals facilitates the distinction between statistical significance and clinical significance."*
:::

---

## Hypothesis Testing

**Hypothesis testing** is a formal way of using data (evidence) to answer research questions. It ensures that — in the long run — we reach the correct conclusion a certain percentage of the time.

### Steps in Hypothesis Testing

1. **State the hypotheses** — null ($H_0$) and alternative ($H_1$).
2. **Set the significance level** ($\alpha$, usually 0.05).
3. **Collect data and compute a test statistic**.
4. **Calculate the p-value**.
5. **Make a decision** — reject or fail to reject $H_0$.
6. **State your conclusion** in terms of the population parameter, and always quote the p-value.

> *Professor's note:* *"When you are doing any reports or anything, you should always quote your p-value in your conclusions. And remember, when you're drawing your conclusions, if you're doing hypothesis testing, your conclusions are about the population parameter that you're studying."*

---

### The Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)

The **null hypothesis** is the starting point — a statement of "no effect" or "no difference". We assume it is true until the data gives us enough evidence to doubt it.

- Always stated with an **equality sign**: $H_0: \mu = 0$
- The statement is made about the **population parameter**, not the sample statistic.

> *Professor's note:* *"We assume this statement is true until the data indicates otherwise."*

---

### The Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)

The **alternative hypothesis** is your **research theory** — what you believe is actually true.

- It contains all values **not** covered by the null hypothesis.
- Together, $H_0$ and $H_1$ must cover all possible situations.

| Type | Hypotheses | Called |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Two-sided | $H_0: \mu = 0$, $H_1: \mu \neq 0$ | Two-tailed test |
| One-sided (greater) | $H_0: \mu = 0$, $H_1: \mu > 0$ | One-tailed test |
| One-sided (less) | $H_0: \mu = 0$, $H_1: \mu < 0$ | One-tailed test |

> *Professor's note:* *"The alternative hypothesis is your research theory. The researcher actually believes this statement to be true."*

---

## The p-value

The **p-value** is the result of the significance test. It tells you how likely the null hypothesis is to be true, given the data you observed.

More precisely: it is the probability of observing data at least as extreme as yours, *if the null hypothesis were true*.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The p-value — Most Important Concept in Hypothesis Testing

- A **small p-value** → the null hypothesis is unlikely to be true → we have **strong evidence against** $H_0$.
- Always report the exact p-value in your conclusions.

> 🎵 *Professor's classroom rhyme (do not write in exams!):*
> **"If p is low, null must go."**
> That is: if $p \leq \alpha$ (e.g., $p \leq 0.05$), **reject** $H_0$.

The **only two valid conclusions** you can write are:

1. *"We reject the null hypothesis."* (when $p \leq \alpha$)
2. *"We fail to reject the null hypothesis."* (when $p > \alpha$)

**Anything else is incorrect.**

> *Professor's note:* *"When I teach hypothesis testing, there's only two statements that you're allowed to use. Everything else is incorrect."*
:::

---

## Level of Significance ($\alpha$)

The **significance level** $\alpha$ is set **before** the test is run. It is the amount of error you are willing to accept in your conclusion.

| $\alpha$ | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| 0.05 (5%) | Most common — you accept a 5% chance of a Type I error |
| 0.01 (1%) | More stringent — used when false positives are very costly |
| 0.001 (0.1%) | Very stringent — used in fields like genetics |

**Decision rule:**

- If $p \leq \alpha$: **Reject $H_0$** → result is **statistically significant**
- If $p > \alpha$: **Fail to reject $H_0$** → not sufficient evidence

> *Professor's note:* *"This is the degree of uncertainty we are prepared to accept in a conclusion. It depends on your study."*

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common Mistakes

- A **significant** result does **not** mean the result is certainly true — only that we have evidence.
- A **non-significant** result does **not** mean the null hypothesis is true — only that we do not have enough evidence to reject it.
- Never say: *"We accept $H_0$"* or *"We accept $H_1$"*. These are **wrong**.
:::

---

## Type I and Type II Errors

Because we base conclusions on sample data, we can sometimes make mistakes.

| | $H_0$ is actually **TRUE** | $H_0$ is actually **FALSE** |
|---|---|---|
| **We Reject $H_0$** | ❌ **Type I Error** (probability = $\alpha$) | ✅ Correct — this is **Power** |
| **We Fail to Reject $H_0$** | ✅ Correct | ❌ **Type II Error** (probability = $\beta$) |

- **Type I Error** = "False positive" — rejecting a true null hypothesis. You can control this by setting $\alpha$.
- **Type II Error** = "False negative" — failing to detect a real effect. Its probability is $\beta$. You cannot directly control it without knowing the true population value.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Key Point on Errors

We can **control** Type I errors by setting $\alpha$. We **cannot directly control** Type II errors — because to calculate the exact probability of $\beta$, we would need to know the true population parameter, which is unknown.

> *Professor's note:* *"We controlled for Type 1 errors by setting the level of significance, your alpha, or your 5%, or your 1%."*
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Real-World Analogy

A Type I Error is like a **false fire alarm** — the alarm goes off (you reject $H_0$) but there is no fire (no real effect). A Type II Error is like a **missed fire** — there is a real fire (a real effect), but the alarm does not go off (you fail to detect it).
:::

---

## Statistical Power

**Power** is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — of rejecting $H_0$ when it truly is false.

$$
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta
$$

- **High power** is desirable — you want your study to have a good chance of finding an effect if one really exists.
- **The trade-off:** Decreasing $\alpha$ (being more careful about Type I errors) automatically *increases* $\beta$ (makes Type II errors more likely), which *reduces* power.

### Increasing Power

The best solution to this trade-off is to **increase the sample size** before the study begins. With enough data, you can keep both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at acceptable levels simultaneously.

```{r power-example, eval=FALSE}
# Install packages if needed
# install.packages("pwr")
# install.packages("asbio")

library(pwr)
library(asbio)

# Q6: One-sample test — power of detecting a 0.5 hr difference
# sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, n = 50, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.5,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q6d: Minimum sample size for 90% power, detecting 15-minute (0.25 hr) difference
power.z.test(sigma = 1.35, power = 0.9, alpha = 0.05, effect = 0.25,
             test = "two.tail", strict = FALSE)

# Q7a: Two-sample t-test — power, n=20 per group, pooled SD=6.2, effect=5 complaints
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7b: Recalculate with alpha = 0.01
pwr.t.test(n = 20, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.01,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q7c: Minimum sample size for 90% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.9, d = (5/6.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Q8c: HbA1c intervention — one-sample, one-sided, 80% power
pwr.t.test(power = 0.8, d = (1/2.2), sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "less")
```

> *Professor's note:* *"We can select a sample size before the study commences so that alpha and beta are both at satisfactory levels."*

---

## Effect Size

The **effect size** tells you how **big or strong** an effect is — separate from whether it is statistically significant.

A result can be statistically significant but practically tiny. The effect size tells you if the result actually matters in the real world.

- **Larger effect size** = stronger relationship = more likely to be practically important
- Associated with every effect size is a **standard error** (a measure of its uncertainty)

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Why Effect Size is Important

> *Professor's note:* *"It's good in practice to report your effect size. You're doing it anyway — you don't know you're doing it when you're reporting a confidence interval."*

Reporting a confidence interval is a way of reporting effect size, even if you do not realise it.
:::

### Cohen's d (for t-tests)

Used when comparing two means:

$$
d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}}
$$

The difference between two means, divided by the pooled standard deviation. This standardises the difference so it can be compared across studies.

### $R^2$ (for Linear Regression)

In linear regression, effect size is measured by $R^2$ — the square of Pearson's correlation coefficient $r$:

$$
R^2 = r^2
$$

$R^2$ is the **coefficient of determination**. It tells you what proportion of the variation in $Y$ is explained by $X$.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ How to Interpret $R^2$

> *Professor's note:* *"If you had a coefficient of determination that was 40%, the interpretation would be: 40% of the variation in Y is explained by the variation in X. Which leaves 60% — that's unexplained variation, maybe due to something else you haven't considered."*

**Always express $R^2$ as a percentage** when interpreting it, and identify what the unexplained variation might be due to.
:::

---

## Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance

This is one of the most important distinctions in applied statistics.

| | Statistical Significance | Clinical / Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **What it means** | Evidence that an effect exists in the data | The effect is large enough to matter in practice |
| **How it is judged** | p-value vs. $\alpha$ | Effect size |
| **Risk when sample is large** | Small, trivial effects may be "significant" | May still have very small effect size |
| **Risk when sample is small** | A real effect may not be detected | Under-powered study may miss important differences |

**For a result to be truly meaningful, it must be:**

1. First — **statistically significant** ($p \leq \alpha$)
2. Then — have a **meaningful effect size** (large enough to matter practically)

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ The Blood Pressure Example

> *Professor's note:* *"If you're studying blood pressure — small changes in blood pressure might be practically significant. It depends on the subject that you're studying."*

In medicine, a statistically significant reduction of even 2 mmHg in blood pressure across a large population could prevent thousands of strokes. So "small" effect sizes can still be practically important — it always depends on **context**.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common Mistake: Over-relying on the p-value

> *Professor's note:* *"Instead of attaching too much importance on statistical significance, we should pay attention to the actual data as well as the p-value."*

- A **non-significant result** does NOT mean there is no effect. It means you did not have enough evidence to detect one (perhaps your sample size was too small).
- A **significant result** with a tiny effect may have little practical value.
- Always interpret **both** the p-value and the confidence interval together.
:::

---

# Exercise Sheet Summary

This table maps the main exercises to the concepts they test.

| Question | Topic | Key Concept |
|----------|-------|------------|
| Q1 | p-value | What exactly does a p-value tell you? |
| Q2 | Type II error | Definition of Type II error |
| Q3 | Power & sample size | Effect of increasing n on Type II error probability |
| Q4 | Bias | Definition of an unbiased estimator |
| Q5 | CLT simulation | Visualising how sample mean distribution becomes Normal |
| Q6 | Power analysis | One-sample z-test; power calculation by hand and in R |
| Q7 | Power analysis | Two-sample t-test; power and minimum sample size |
| Q8 | Power analysis | Clinical trial; HbA1c; one-sided test; Type I/II interpretation |
| Q9 | Sample size for CI | Margin of error: battery life, $n$ for 95% CI |
| Q10 | Sample size for CI | Margin of error: sugar content, $n$ for 99% CI |
| Q11 | Sample size for CI | Margin of error: flight delays, $n$ for 99% CI |

### Sample Size for a Confidence Interval (Questions 9–11)

When you want to estimate a population mean with a specific **margin of error** $E$ and confidence level, the required sample size formula is:

$$
n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2
$$

Where:

- $z_{\alpha/2}$ = critical value (1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%)
- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation
- $E$ = desired margin of error

Always **round up** to the nearest whole number.

---

### Practice Question with Answer

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Practice: State H₀ and H₁

A company targets a mean order processing time of 6 hours. A sample of 50 orders had a mean of 5.2 hours and standard deviation of 1.35 hours. State the hypotheses.

**Answer:**

$$H_0: \mu = 6 \text{ hours}$$

$$H_1: \mu \neq 6 \text{ hours}$$

Note: $H_0$ uses an **equality sign** and refers to the **population mean** $\mu$, not the sample mean $\bar{x}$.
:::

---

# Key Formulas Reference

| Formula | What It Is |
|---------|-----------|
| $\text{SEM} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Standard Error of the Mean |
| $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu,\ \sigma/\sqrt{n})$ | Sampling distribution (Normal population) |
| $\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot (\sigma/\sqrt{n})$ | Approximate 95% Confidence Interval |
| $\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$ | Power of a test |
| $d = (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) / s_{\text{pooled}}$ | Cohen's d (effect size for t-tests) |
| $R^2 = r^2$ | Coefficient of determination (effect size for regression) |
| $n = (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^2$ | Sample size for estimating a mean |

---

# Summary Checklist

- ✅ I can explain the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics.
- ✅ I can distinguish between a population parameter and a sample statistic.
- ✅ I know why random (probability) sampling is essential.
- ✅ I can define and calculate the Standard Error of the Mean (SEM).
- ✅ I can state the Central Limit Theorem and explain what it means in plain language.
- ✅ I can explain the logic of a confidence interval.
- ✅ I can state a null and alternative hypothesis correctly.
- ✅ I know what a p-value tells me and how to use it to make a decision.
- ✅ I know the two and only two valid conclusions in hypothesis testing.
- ✅ I can define Type I and Type II errors and explain which one we can control.
- ✅ I can define statistical power and explain the trade-off between $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
- ✅ I can explain what effect size is and name two measures: Cohen's d and $R^2$.
- ✅ I can interpret $R^2$ as a percentage (e.g., "40% of the variation in Y is explained by X").
- ✅ I can distinguish between statistical significance and clinical/practical significance.
- ✅ I always report the p-value and a confidence interval together in my conclusions.

---

# Pre-Topic Checklist: Before Moving to Week 3

- [ ] I can calculate the SEM by hand for given values of $\sigma$ and $n$.
- [ ] I can explain why the CLT is important and when it applies ($n \geq 30$).
- [ ] I can write a pair of hypotheses ($H_0$ and $H_1$) for a given research question.
- [ ] Given a p-value and $\alpha$, I can make the correct decision and write the correct conclusion statement.
- [ ] I understand the difference between Type I and Type II errors and can give an example of each.
- [ ] I can explain power ($1 - \beta$) and describe how to increase it.
- [ ] I can use the `pwr` package in R to calculate power and minimum sample sizes.
- [ ] I can calculate the required sample size for a confidence interval using the margin of error formula.
- [ ] I can interpret $R^2$ and explain what the "unexplained" variation represents.
- [ ] I can correctly interpret a confidence interval — I do NOT say "there is a 95% probability that $\mu$ is in this interval" (incorrect), but rather that "95% of such intervals would contain the true mean".

---

# Additional Resources

- R packages used: `pwr`, `asbio` — install with `install.packages("pwr")` and `install.packages("asbio")`
- R script for Exercise 2 is available on Canvas (under Units → Regression Analysis)
- *Fethney (2010)* paper on statistical and clinical significance — available in the course materials

---
